# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of the Normal Distribution $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$

The Normal Distribution is uniquely characterized by the following structural properties. Each property is a **board-exam favorite** and must be memorized.

- **P1 вҖ” Symmetry:** The PDF $f_X(x)$ is perfectly symmetric about the vertical line $x = \mu$. Mathematically, $f_X(\mu + a) = f_X(\mu - a)$ for all $a$.
- **P2 вҖ” Unimodality:** The curve has exactly **one peak** (one local maximum), located at $x = \mu$. This makes $\mu$ simultaneously the **mean, median, and mode**.
- **P3 вҖ” Asymptotic Tails:** As $x \to \pm\infty$, the exponential term $e^{-(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2}$ decays to zero *faster than any polynomial*. The tails never touch the x-axis.
- **P4 вҖ” Total Probability:** The total area under the PDF is exactly 1:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \, dx = 1$$
- **P5 вҖ” Inflection Points:** The curve changes concavity at $x = \mu - \sigma$ and $x = \mu + \sigma$. These are the "shoulders" of the bell.
- **P6 вҖ” Mean and Variance:** $E[X] = \mu$ and $\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2$.
- **P7 вҖ” Location-Scale Family:** If $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ and $Y = aX + b$, then $Y \sim N(a\mu + b, \, a^2\sigma^2)$.
- **P8 вҖ” Reproductive Property:** If $X_1 \sim N(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2)$ and $X_2 \sim N(\mu_2, \sigma_2^2)$ are **independent**, then $X_1 + X_2 \sim N(\mu_1 + \mu_2, \, \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2)$.

## 2.2 Properties of the Standard Normal Distribution $Z \sim N(0, 1)$

- **P9 вҖ” Standardization:** If $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma} \sim N(0,1)$.
- **P10 вҖ” Symmetry of $\Phi$:** $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ вҖ” a **critical identity** for the KTU exam.
- **P11 вҖ” Reciprocal Complements:** $P(Z > z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ and $P(-z \leq Z \leq z) = 2\Phi(z) - 1$.
- **P12 вҖ” PDF even function:** $\phi(-z) = \phi(z)$.
- **P13 вҖ” Odd Moment Vanishing:** All odd moments of $Z$ are zero: $E[Z^{2k+1}] = 0$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$
- **P14 вҖ” Even Moments:** $E[Z^{2k}] = (2k-1)!! = 1 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 \cdots (2k-1)$.

## 2.3 The Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 Rule)

For any Normal Distribution $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$:

| Interval | Probability | Decimal |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| $\mu - \sigma \leq X \leq \mu + \sigma$ | $P$ | **вүҲ 0.6827** |
| $\mu - 2\sigma \leq X \leq \mu + 2\sigma$ | $P$ | **вүҲ 0.9545** |
| $\mu - 3\sigma \leq X \leq \mu + 3\sigma$ | $P$ | **вүҲ 0.9973** |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Empirical Rule is used heavily in **Six-Sigma quality control**, **manufacturing tolerances**, and **biostatistics** (e.g., pediatric growth charts). A measurement beyond $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ is considered an outlier with probability less than **0.3%**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet вҖ” Print This Section Before the Exam**

| # | Formula / Identity | Expression | Used For |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Normal PDF | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2}$ | Defining $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ |
| 2 | Standard Normal PDF | $\phi(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-z^2/2}$ | Defining $Z \sim N(0,1)$ |
| 3 | Standard Normal CDF | $\Phi(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{z} \phi(t)\,dt$ | Finding $P(Z \leq z)$ |
| 4 | Z-Transformation | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Converting $X$ to $Z$ |
| 5 | Inverse Transformation | $X = \mu + Z\sigma$ | Converting $Z$ back to $X$ |
| 6 | Symmetry Identity | $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ | Negative $z$ values |
| 7 | Complement Rule | $P(Z > z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ | Right-tail probability |
| 8 | Two-sided Probability | $P(-z \leq Z \leq z) = 2\Phi(z) - 1$ | Symmetric intervals |
| 9 | General Interval | $P(a \leq Z \leq b) = \Phi(b) - \Phi(a)$ | Any interval |
| 10 | General $X$ Interval | $P(a \leq X \leq b) = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{b-\mu}{\sigma}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{a-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | Non-standard $X$ |
| 11 | Linear Transformation | $Y = aX + b \Rightarrow Y \sim N(a\mu+b, \, a^2\sigma^2)$ | New mean/variance |
| 12 | Sum of Independent | $X_1 + X_2 \sim N(\mu_1+\mu_2, \, \sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2)$ | Reproductive property |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exam Pitfall:** When asked to find $P(\vert Z \vert \leq z)$ or $P(\vert X - \mu \vert \leq k)$, students often forget to **double** the one-sided probability. The correct formula is $P(\vert Z \vert \leq z) = 2\Phi(z) - 1$, not just $\Phi(z)$. This single error costs **3 marks** on a typical KTU problem.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Biostatistics / Life Sciences:** Modeling blood pressure, cholesterol levels, birth weights, and drug response curves. The KTU GDMAT301 course places special emphasis on this.
- **Quality Control (Six-Sigma):** Manufacturing tolerances in semiconductor fabrication use the $6\sigma$ rule to minimize defects.
- **Signal Processing:** Gaussian noise is the standard noise model in communication systems.
- **Finance:** Stock returns are approximately normally distributed (with fat tails).
- **Psychometrics:** IQ scores are *deliberately* designed to follow $N(100, 15^2)$.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation вҖ” Mean of the Standard Normal Distribution

We must show $E[Z] = 0$. By definition:

$$E[Z] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \, \phi(z) \, dz = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-z^2/2} \, dz$$

**Step 1 вҖ” Identify the integrand's parity.** The function $z \cdot e^{-z^2/2}$ is the product of an odd function ($z$) and an even function ($e^{-z^2/2}$), which is **odd**.

**Step 2 вҖ” Apply the odd-function integral theorem.** For any odd function $g(z)$ integrated over the symmetric interval $(-\infty, \infty)$:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(z) \, dz = 0$$

This is because the integral from $-\infty$ to $0$ cancels exactly with the integral from $0$ to $\infty$.

**Step 3 вҖ” Conclude.**

$$\boxed{E[Z] = 0}$$

## 3.2 Derivation вҖ” Variance of the Standard Normal Distribution

We need $\text{Var}(Z) = E[Z^2] - (E[Z])^2 = E[Z^2]$.

**Step 1 вҖ” Set up the integral.**

$$E[Z^2] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-z^2/2} \, dz = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z^2 \, e^{-z^2/2} \, dz$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Integration by parts.** Let $u = z$ and $dv = z \, e^{-z^2/2} \, dz$. Then $du = dz$ and $v = -e^{-z^2/2}$.

$$E[Z^2] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \left[ \left(-z \, e^{-z^2/2}\right)\Big|_{-\infty}^{\infty} + \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-z^2/2} \, dz \right]$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Evaluate the boundary term.** As $z \to \pm\infty$, $z \, e^{-z^2/2} \to 0$ because the exponential dominates. The boundary term vanishes.

**Step 4 вҖ” Recognize the remaining integral.** The remaining integral is the famous **Gaussian integral**:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-z^2/2} \, dz = \sqrt{2\pi}$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Substitute back.**

$$E[Z^2] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{2\pi} = 1$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Conclude the variance.**

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(Z) = E[Z^2] - (E[Z])^2 = 1 - 0^2 = 1}$$

## 3.3 Derivation вҖ” Mean and Variance of $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$

**Step 1 вҖ” Standardize.** Let $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$, so $X = \mu + \sigma Z$.

**Step 2 вҖ” Compute $E[X]$ using linearity.**

$$E[X] = E[\mu + \sigma Z] = \mu + \sigma \cdot E[Z] = \mu + \sigma \cdot 0 = \mu$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Compute $\text{Var}(X)$ using scaling rules.**

$$\text{Var}(X) = \text{Var}(\mu + \sigma Z) = \sigma^2 \cdot \text{Var}(Z) = \sigma^2 \cdot 1 = \sigma^2$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{E[X] = \mu \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 3.4.1 вҖ” Basic Standard Normal Probability

**Problem:** Find $P(Z \leq 1.5)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 вҖ” Recognize the form.** This is a direct lookup of the Standard Normal CDF at $z = 1.5$.

**Step 2 вҖ” Read from the Z-table.**

$$\Phi(1.5) = 0.9332$$

**Step 3 вҖ” State the final answer.**

$$\boxed{P(Z \leq 1.5) = 0.9332}$$

---

### Example 3.4.2 вҖ” Symmetry Identity Application

**Problem:** Find $P(Z \leq -0.75)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 вҖ” Use the symmetry identity** $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$.

**Step 2 вҖ” Substitute $z = 0.75$.**

$$\Phi(-0.75) = 1 - \Phi(0.75)$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Look up $\Phi(0.75) = 0.7734$ from the Z-table.**

$$\Phi(-0.75) = 1 - 0.7734 = 0.2266$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Final answer.**

$$\boxed{P(Z \leq -0.75) = 0.2266}$$

---

### Example 3.4.3 вҖ” Two-Sided Probability

**Problem:** Find $P(-1.2 \leq Z \leq 2.1)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 вҖ” Apply the general interval formula.**

$$P(a \leq Z \leq b) = \Phi(b) - \Phi(a)$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Substitute $a = -1.2$ and $b = 2.1$.**

$$P(-1.2 \leq Z \leq 2.1) = \Phi(2.1) - \Phi(-1.2)$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Use the symmetry identity on the negative argument.**

$$\Phi(-1.2) = 1 - \Phi(1.2) = 1 - 0.8849 = 0.1151$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Look up $\Phi(2.1) = 0.9821$.**

**Step 5 вҖ” Combine.**

$$P(-1.2 \leq Z \leq 2.1) = 0.9821 - 0.1151 = 0.8670$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Final answer.**

$$\boxed{P(-1.2 \leq Z \leq 2.1) = 0.8670}$$

---

### Example 3.4.4 вҖ” Converting Non-Standard Normal to Standard Normal

**Problem:** If $X \sim N(50, 25)$ (i.e., $\mu = 50, \sigma = 5$), find $P(45 \leq X \leq 60)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 вҖ” Apply the Z-transformation to both endpoints.**

$$z_1 = \frac{45 - 50}{5} = \frac{-5}{5} = -1.00$$

$$z_2 = \frac{60 - 50}{5} = \frac{10}{5} = 2.00$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Rewrite the probability in terms of $Z$.**

$$P(45 \leq X \leq 60) = P(-1.00 \leq Z \leq 2.00)$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Use the interval formula.**

$$P(-1.00 \leq Z \leq 2.00) = \Phi(2.00) - \Phi(-1.00)$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Look up values.** $\Phi(2.00) = 0.9772$, $\Phi(-1.00) = 1 - \Phi(1.00) = 1 - 0.8413 = 0.1587$.

**Step 5 вҖ” Compute the result.**

$$P(-1.00 \leq Z \leq 2.00) = 0.9772 - 0.1587 = 0.8185$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Final answer.**

$$\boxed{P(45 \leq X \leq 60) = 0.8185}$$

---

### Example 3.4.5 вҖ” Finding the Value of $X$ Given a Probability (Inverse Problem)

**Problem:** If $X \sim N(100, 16)$ (i.e., $\mu = 100, \sigma = 4$), find the value $x$ such that $P(X \leq x) = 0.95$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 вҖ” Standardize both sides.**

$$P\!\left(\frac{X - 100}{4} \leq \frac{x - 100}{4}\right) = 0.95$$

$$P\!\left(Z \leq \frac{x - 100}{4}\right) = 0.95$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Equate to the standard normal CDF.**

$$\Phi\!\left(\frac{x - 100}{4}\right) = 0.95$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Look up the inverse.** We need the $z$-value such that $\Phi(z) = 0.95$. From the Z-table, $z = 1.645$.

**Step 4 вҖ” Solve for $x$.**

$$\frac{x - 100}{4} = 1.645$$

$$x = 100 + 4 \times 1.645 = 100 + 6.58 = 106.58$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Final answer.**

$$\boxed{x = 106.58}$$

---

### Example 3.4.6 вҖ” Empirical Rule Application

**Problem:** The marks of KTU students in an exam follow $N(65, 100)$ (i.e., $\mu = 65, \sigma = 10$). What percentage of students score between 55 and 85?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 вҖ” Identify the interval in terms of $\sigma$.**

- Lower bound: $55 = 65 - 10 = \mu - 1\sigma$
- Upper bound: $85 = 65 + 20 = \mu + 2\sigma$

**Step 2 вҖ” Convert to Z-scores.**

$$z_1 = \frac{55 - 65}{10} = -1.00$$

$$z_2 = \frac{85 - 65}{10} = 2.00$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Compute the probability using the table.**

$$P(-1.00 \leq Z \leq 2.00) = \Phi(2.00) - \Phi(-1.00) = 0.9772 - 0.1587 = 0.8185$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Convert to percentage.**

$$\boxed{\approx 81.85\% \text{ of students score between 55 and 85}}$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram вҖ” Functional Architecture of Normal Distribution Computations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Random Variable X] --> B{Is X Standard Normal?}
    B -- "Yes: Z ~ N0,1" --> C[Read Phi-z directly from Z-table]
    B -- "No: X ~ Nmu,sigma2" --> D[Apply Z-Transformation]
    D --> E["Z = X minus mu over sigma"]
    E --> F[Rewrite Probability in terms of Z]
    F --> G{Probability Type?}
    G -- "Cumulative P of Z le z" --> H[Look up Phi-z in Z-table]
    G -- "Interval: P of a le Z le b" --> I["Phi of b minus Phi of a"]
    G -- "Two-sided: P of vert Z vert le c" --> J["2 times Phi of c minus 1"]
    G -- "Inverse: find x given P" --> K[Invert Phi to find z critical]
    K --> L["x equals mu plus z times sigma"]
    H --> M[Final Numerical Answer]
    I --> M
    J --> M
    C --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Verify using Empirical Rule sanity check]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Conceptual Map вҖ” Relationships between Normal, Standard Normal, and Key Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N[Normal Distribution X ~ N of mu, sigma2] -- "Z = X-mu over sigma" --> S[Standard Normal Z ~ N of 0, 1]
    S -- "phi of z equals 1 over sqrt 2pi times e to the minus z squared over 2" --> PDF[PDF phi of z]
    S -- "Phi of z equals integral phi of t dt" --> CDF[CDF Phi of z]
    CDF -- "Phi of minus z equals 1 minus Phi of z" --> SYM[Symmetry Identity]
    N -- "Symmetric about x = mu" --> SYM1[Symmetry Property]
    N -- "Mean equals Median equals Mode equals mu" --> CTR[Central Tendency]
    N -- "P within mu +- sigma approx 0.6827" --> ER[Empirical Rule 68-95-99.7]
    N -- "Y = aX + b leads to N of amu+b, a2 sigma2" --> LS[Location-Scale Property]
    N -- "Sum of independent Normals is Normal" --> RP[Reproductive Property]
```

## 4.3 Schematic вҖ” The Anatomy of the Bell Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[Peak: x equals mu, Mean equals Median equals Mode] --> SIG1[Right Inflection: x equals mu + sigma]
    SIG1 --> SIG2[Right Tail: x approaches infinity, f of x approaches 0]
    TOP --> SIG1L[Left Inflection: x equals mu - sigma]
    SIG1L --> SIG2L[Left Tail: x approaches minus infinity, f of x approaches 0]
    SIG1 --> R1["68.27 percent of area between mu - sigma and mu + sigma"]
    SIG1 --> R2["95.45 percent of area between mu - 2sigma and mu + 2sigma"]
    SIG1 --> R3["99.73 percent of area between mu - 3sigma and mu + 3sigma"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 вҖ” `[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Question, Module 4]`

**Define the Normal Distribution. State any four of its properties.**

**Model Answer:**

A continuous random variable $X$ is said to follow a Normal Distribution with parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ if its probability density function is:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{\, -(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2} \quad , \quad -\infty < x < \infty$$

We write $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

**Four Properties:**

1. The curve is **symmetric** about the line $x = \mu$, so $\mu$ is the mean, median, and mode.
2. The **total area** under the curve is 1: $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$.
3. The **inflection points** occur at $x = \mu - \sigma$ and $x = \mu + \sigma$.
4. The variance is $\sigma^2$, and the standard deviation is $\sigma$.

---

### Question A2 вҖ” `[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Question, Module 4]`

**What is the Standard Normal Distribution? State the Z-transformation formula.**

**Model Answer:**

The Standard Normal Distribution is a special case of the Normal Distribution with mean $\mu = 0$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 1$. We denote it $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ with PDF:

$$\phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{\, -z^2/2} \quad , \quad -\infty < z < \infty$$

**Z-Transformation Formula:**

If $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma} \sim N(0, 1)$.

This transformation **standardizes** any Normal random variable into the Standard Normal, allowing use of the Z-table.

---

## 5.2 Part B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **one full question** out of the two choices provided.

---

### Question B-A вҖ” `[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Question, Module 4]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the mean and variance of the Standard Normal Distribution $Z \sim N(0,1)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If $X \sim N(40, 36)$ (i.e., $\mu = 40, \sigma = 6$), find:
- (i) $P(X \leq 50)$
- (ii) $P(35 \leq X \leq 48)$
- (iii) The value of $x$ such that $P(X \leq x) = 0.90$ **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B-A (a):

**Mean Derivation:**

$$E[Z] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2} \, dz$$

The integrand $z \cdot e^{-z^2/2}$ is an **odd function**, so the integral over $(-\infty, \infty)$ is **0**.

$$E[Z] = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Variance Derivation:**

$$\text{Var}(Z) = E[Z^2] - (E[Z])^2 = E[Z^2]$$

$$E[Z^2] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2} \, dz$$

Using integration by parts with $u = z$ and $dv = z \, e^{-z^2/2}\,dz$ (so $v = -e^{-z^2/2}$):

$$E[Z^2] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \left[ -z \, e^{-z^2/2} \Big|_{-\infty}^{\infty} + \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-z^2/2} \, dz \right]$$

The boundary term is 0 (exponential decay). The remaining Gaussian integral equals $\sqrt{2\pi}$.

$$E[Z^2] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{2\pi} = 1$$

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(Z) = 1 - 0^2 = 1}$$

---

#### Model Solution for B-A (b):

Given: $X \sim N(40, 36) \Rightarrow \mu = 40, \sigma = 6$. The Z-transformation is $Z = \dfrac{X - 40}{6}$.

**(i) Find $P(X \leq 50)$:**

**Step 1:** Standardize the boundary.

$$z = \frac{50 - 40}{6} = \frac{10}{6} = 1.67$$

**Step 2:** Read from the Z-table.

$$\Phi(1.67) = 0.9525$$

**Step 3:** Conclude.

$$\boxed{P(X \leq 50) = 0.9525}$$

---

**(ii) Find $P(35 \leq X \leq 48)$:**

**Step 1:** Standardize both bounds.

$$z_1 = \frac{35 - 40}{6} = \frac{-5}{6} = -0.83$$

$$z_2 = \frac{48 - 40}{6} = \frac{8}{6} = 1.33$$

**Step 2:** Apply the interval formula.

$$P(35 \leq X \leq 48) = \Phi(1.33) - \Phi(-0.83)$$

**Step 3:** Use the symmetry identity.

$$\Phi(-0.83) = 1 - \Phi(0.83) = 1 - 0.7967 = 0.2033$$

**Step 4:** Look up $\Phi(1.33) = 0.9082$.

**Step 5:** Compute.

$$P(35 \leq X \leq 48) = 0.9082 - 0.2033 = 0.7049$$

$$\boxed{P(35 \leq X \leq 48) = 0.7049}$$

---

**(iii) Find $x$ such that $P(X \leq x) = 0.90$:**

**Step 1:** Standardize.

$$P\!\left(Z \leq \frac{x - 40}{6}\right) = 0.90$$

**Step 2:** Find $z$ such that $\Phi(z) = 0.90$. From the Z-table, $z = 1.28$.

**Step 3:** Solve for $x$.

$$\frac{x - 40}{6} = 1.28$$

$$x = 40 + 6 \times 1.28 = 40 + 7.68 = 47.68$$

$$\boxed{x = 47.68}$$

---

### Question B-B вҖ” `[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Question, Module 4]` (14 Marks) [INTERNAL CHOICE]

**(a)** State and prove the symmetry property of the Standard Normal Distribution: $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The systolic blood pressure of men in a certain age group is normally distributed with mean $\mu = 125$ mmHg and standard deviation $\sigma = 10$ mmHg. Find the probability that a randomly selected man has blood pressure:
- (i) Between 110 and 140 mmHg
- (ii) Greater than 145 mmHg
- (iii) Less than 105 mmHg **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B-B (a):

**Statement:** For any real number $z$, $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 вҖ” Express $\Phi(-z)$ as an integral.**

$$\Phi(-z) = \int_{-\infty}^{-z} \phi(t) \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{-z} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-t^2/2} \, dt$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Substitute $u = -t$, so $t = -u$ and $dt = -du$. When $t = -\infty$, $u = \infty$; when $t = -z$, $u = z$.**

$$\Phi(-z) = \int_{\infty}^{z} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-u^2/2} \cdot (-du) = \int_{z}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-u^2/2} \, du$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Recognize the complement.**

$$\int_{z}^{\infty} \phi(u) \, du = 1 - \int_{-\infty}^{z} \phi(u) \, du = 1 - \Phi(z)$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Conclude.**

$$\boxed{\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

#### Model Solution for B-B (b):

Given: $X \sim N(125, 100)$, so $\mu = 125$ and $\sigma = 10$. The Z-transformation is $Z = \dfrac{X - 125}{10}$.

**(i) $P(110 \leq X \leq 140)$:**

**Step 1:** Standardize both bounds.

$$z_1 = \frac{110 - 125}{10} = \frac{-15}{10} = -1.50$$

$$z_2 = \frac{140 - 125}{10} = \frac{15}{10} = 1.50$$

**Step 2:** Apply the interval formula.

$$P(110 \leq X \leq 140) = \Phi(1.50) - \Phi(-1.50)$$

**Step 3:** Use symmetry: $\Phi(-1.50) = 1 - \Phi(1.50) = 1 - 0.9332 = 0.0668$.

**Step 4:** Compute.

$$P(110 \leq X \leq 140) = 0.9332 - 0.0668 = 0.8664$$

$$\boxed{P(110 \leq X \leq 140) = 0.8664}$$

---

**(ii) $P(X > 145)$:**

**Step 1:** Standardize.

$$z = \frac{145 - 125}{10} = \frac{20}{10} = 2.00$$

**Step 2:** Use the complement rule.

$$P(X > 145) = P(Z > 2.00) = 1 - \Phi(2.00) = 1 - 0.9772 = 0.0228$$

$$\boxed{P(X > 145) = 0.0228}$$

---

**(iii) $P(X < 105)$:**

**Step 1:** Standardize.

$$z = \frac{105 - 125}{10} = \frac{-20}{10} = -2.00$$

**Step 2:** Use the symmetry identity.

$$P(X < 105) = \Phi(-2.00) = 1 - \Phi(2.00) = 1 - 0.9772 = 0.0228$$

$$\boxed{P(X < 105) = 0.0228}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall вҖ” Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Arithmetic errors in Z-transformation:** Students frequently compute $\sigma = 10$ when the question gives $\sigma^2 = 100$, or vice versa. Always extract $\mu$ and $\sigma$ (not $\sigma^2$) first.
> 2. **Forgetting to use symmetry on negative Z-values:** If the standardized value is negative, you MUST apply $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$. Reading the Z-table directly for negative values is a **-2 mark** error in valuation.
> 3. **Using $\Phi(z)$ when $P(Z > z)$ is asked:** Always convert "greater than" to "less than" using the complement rule: $P(Z > z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$.
> 4. **Two-sided probability halved:** For $P(\vert Z \vert \leq z)$, students often write just $\Phi(z)$ instead of $2\Phi(z) - 1$. This is a **3-mark** deduction in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 5. **Not stating the units or final boxed answer:** KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for unboxed or unlabelled final answers in numerical problems.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist вҖ” Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- **Definition of Normal Distribution:** $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2}$, denoted $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.
- **Definition of Standard Normal:** $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ with $\phi(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}$.
- **Z-Transformation (MOST IMPORTANT):** $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$.
- **Inverse Transformation:** $X = \mu + Z\sigma$.
- **Symmetry Identity (GOLDEN RULE):** $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$.
- **Complement Rule:** $P(Z > z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$.
- **Two-sided Probability:** $P(-z \leq Z \leq z) = 2\Phi(z) - 1$.
- **General Interval:** $P(a \leq Z \leq b) = \Phi(b) - \Phi(a)$.
- **Empirical Rule:** 68.27% within $\mu \pm \sigma$, 95.45% within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$, 99.73% within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$.
- **Key Z-values to memorize:** $z = 1.645$ (90% CI), $z = 1.96$ (95% CI), $z = 2.576$ (99% CI).
- **Mean = Median = Mode = $\mu$** for any Normal Distribution.
- **Inflection points** of the bell curve are at $x = \mu \pm \sigma$.
- **Linear transformation:** $Y = aX + b \Rightarrow Y \sim N(a\mu + b, a^2\sigma^2)$.
- **Reproductive property:** Sum of independent Normals is Normal with parameters added.
- **Total area under the curve = 1** вҖ” always.
- **Always extract $\mu$ and $\sigma$ separately** before standardizing. Given "$N(40, 36)$" means $\mu = 40$ and $\sigma^2 = 36$, so $\sigma = 6$.
- **Always BOX the final answer** for full marks in KTU valuation.
